comments.author: @celesterogers885
comments.text: I'm watching from Hawaii, where I've lived for most of my life. However, I grew up in what was once the largest enclave of Hungarians outside of Hungary. That was in Cleveland, Ohio (USA). All of my grandparents were born in Hungary (before Trianon) and died in Cleveland. Btw, your narrator mispronounces almost every Hungarian word he utters. LOL!

comments.author: @dieterbarkhoff1328
comments.text: The 'Finno' language connection has proven to be fiction. Hungarian is unique - a language related to a long-time-ago Turkic/Mongoloid language. My maternal grandmother was ein DonauSchwab, born in Banat, Voivodina, Hungary. In 1919, overnight, it was became, via the Versailles Treaty, Yugoslavia.

comments.author: @zoltanabonyi3307
comments.text: I was born and raised in South Africa to a Hungarian father and a mother with an Irish, Dutch, French and English ancestry. My father came to South Africa as a 12 year old boy with his parents in 1956 during the Hungarian revolution when the Soviets attacked Hungary. They fled to Austria and stayed in army barracks there for 4 months before being put on an American B52 bomber to South Africa. Took 3 days to get there. Landing in Salisbury, Rhodesia first then on to Johannesburg. They arrived with just the shirts on their backs but managed to create a great life in South Africa with my grandfather being highly educated and starting a specialized engineering business which supported the mining and military equipment industry. I still live here on the sub tropical south coast of KwaZulu-Natal. Definately need to experience Hungary as I've never been there. Looks awesome

comments.text: Captivating story being told, all while showing us boring views of  tourist masses and hardly anything other than the streets of modern Budapest. comments.author: @GlobeExperiences2026
comments.text: Hungary is such a fascinating mix of beauty and complex challenges! This video gives an incredible look into life in Hungary, capturing everything from the majestic architecture of Budapest to its vibrant culture. The discussion around Hungary's demographic shift and shrinking population is deeply thought-provoking. It really makes you reflect on the future of Central Europe while appreciating the rich history and charm of the Hungarian people. Fantastic documentary!

comments.author: @georgebardosh1640
comments.text: WATCHING FROM MONTREAL CANADA

replies.author: @AmazingGlobalDiscoveries
replies.text: Welcome! We hope you enjoy the video about Hungary. 😊

comments.author: @GlobeTravel2026
comments.text: is a striking paradox that a small nation of 10 million people produced 16 Nobel Laureates, perfected computing, and created mRNA vaccines, yet is now shrinking by 89 residents every single day. Can a country continue to lead global scientific breakthroughs when its very population base is steadily fading away?

replies.author: @AmazingGlobalDiscoveries
replies.text: That really is one of Hungary’s most powerful paradoxes. A smaller population does not necessarily mean less talent or innovation, but long-term population decline can make it harder to support universities, research institutions, and the next generation of scientists. Hungary’s future may depend on whether it can retain young talent, welcome new people, and create stronger reasons for families to build their lives there

comments.author: @globerealife
comments.text: Building an entire subterranean hospital and nuclear bunker beneath Buda Castle that operated during total warfare shows a level of sheer survival instincts few nations possess.

comments.author: @MathewPanamkat-k6m
comments.text: To keep back people leaving the country, you have to have opportunities of  jobs as per  one's qualifications and expertise. along with salaries matching international standards. Otherwise, citizens will seek foreign shores. Kerala is such a state in India.

replies.author: @AmazingGlobalDiscoveries
replies.text: You’re absolutely right. People are more likely to stay when they can find meaningful work that matches their qualifications, along with fair salaries and a good quality of life. Hungary’s situation has similarities with places like Kerala, where many talented people look abroad for better opportunities.

comments.author: @MathewPanamkat-k6m
comments.text: The chicken and pig habits and their superstitions.- funny!

replies.author: @AmazingGlobalDiscoveries
replies.text: Yes, those traditions and superstitions are both fascinating and quite funny! 😄 They show how everyday animals could become part of local beliefs and customs. Thank you for watching—was there another Hungarian tradition in the video that surprised you?

comments.author: @NICEGLOBE05
comments.text: This documentary captures the stark contrast perfectly. Budapest is easily one of the most majestic, vibrant cities in the world, filled with stunning architecture and Thermal Baths. Yet, just an hour outside the capital, you see the quiet reality of aging villages and youth emigration. It’s a bittersweet reality, but showing both sides is what makes this vlog so authentic and important. Great work!
